vignette: A 45-year-old female presents with symmetric polyarthritis of MCP, PIP, and wrists for 3 months. Morning stiffness lasts 2 hours. Labs: RF positive, Anti-CCP positive. X-rays show periarticular osteopenia and erosions. What is the BEST initial DMARD?

explanation.whyCorrect: RA with erosions = Moderate-severe disease. Methotrexate is FIRST-LINE DMARD (anchor drug). Start 15mg weekly and escalate to 25mg. Always give folic acid to reduce side effects. If inadequate response at 3-6 months → Add biologic (anti-TNF first-line).

whyWrong.A: Steroids are for short-term symptom control while DMARD takes effect (bridge therapy).
whyWrong.C: Hydroxychloroquine is for mild RA or combination therapy, not moderate-severe with erosions.
whyWrong.D: Biologics are second-line after MTX failure (treat-to-target).
whyWrong.E: Sulfasalazine is alternative for mild RA or combination therapy.

explanation.clinicalPearl: RA: Methotrexate first-line (anchor drug). Add biologic if inadequate response. Treat-to-target (remission or low disease activity).

vignette: A 25-year-old African American female presents with malar rash, oral ulcers, arthritis, and pleuritic chest pain. Labs: ANA 1:640, Anti-dsDNA positive, C3/C4 low, Proteinuria 1.5g/day. What is the BEST treatment for her nephritis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Class III/IV lupus nephritis (proteinuria >0.5g/day + active sediment): Induction therapy = Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) + Prednisone (or Cyclophosphamide + Prednisone). MMF is preferred (ALMS trial) due to better safety profile. Maintenance: MMF or Azathioprine.

whyWrong.A: Prednisone alone is insufficient for proliferative nephritis.
whyWrong.C: Cyclophosphamide is alternative but MMF is preferred first-line.
whyWrong.D: Rituximab is for refractory disease (LUNAR trial showed no benefit for induction).
whyWrong.E: Belimumab is add-on therapy for active SLE, not first-line for nephritis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Lupus nephritis Class III/IV: Mycophenolate mofetil + Prednisone (induction) → MMF or AZA (maintenance).

vignette: A 55-year-old obese male presents with acute-onset severe pain, erythema, and swelling of the first MTP joint (podagra). He had a similar episode last year. Joint aspiration shows negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals. What is the BEST treatment for the acute attack?

explanation.whyCorrect: Acute gout attack: Anti-inflammatory therapy first! NSAIDs (naproxen, indomethacin) OR Colchicine (low-dose: 1.2mg then 0.6mg 1h later) OR Steroids (oral or intra-articular). Do NOT start or change urate-lowering therapy (ULT) during acute attack (prolongs flare).

whyWrong.A: Starting ULT during acute attack worsens and prolongs the flare.
whyWrong.C: Never start ULT during acute attack. Treat flare first, then start ULT 2-4 weeks later.
whyWrong.D: Probenecid is for chronic management (under-excretors), not acute attacks.
whyWrong.E: Febuxostat is ULT (second-line), not for acute attacks.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Acute gout: Treat inflammation FIRST (NSAIDs/Colchicine/Steroids). Start ULT (allopurinol) 2-4 weeks AFTER flare resolves.

vignette: A 50-year-old female presents with Raynaud phenomenon, skin thickening on her hands (sclerodactyly), and dysphagia. She has positive Anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I) antibodies. What is the MOST concerning complication?

explanation.whyCorrect: Scleroderma renal crisis: Sudden onset severe HTN + AKI + Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA). Most concerning because it can be RAPIDLY FATAL. Associated with Anti-RNA polymerase III antibody. Treatment: ACE inhibitors (captopril) - LIFE-SAVING. Avoid high-dose steroids (increase risk).

whyWrong.A: ILD is common and serious but progresses slowly.
whyWrong.B: PAH is more common in limited SSc (CREST), not diffuse (anti-Scl70+).
whyWrong.D: Digital ulcers are painful but not acutely life-threatening.
whyWrong.E: Esophageal dysmotility affects quality of life but not mortality.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Scleroderma renal crisis: Sudden HTN + AKI + MAHA. Emergency! ACE inhibitors are life-saving. Check anti-RNA pol III antibody.

vignette: A 25-year-old male presents with chronic low back pain for 2 years. Pain is worse in the morning, improves with exercise, and wakes him at night. He has limited spinal mobility. HLA-B27 is positive. Sacroiliac joint X-ray shows erosions and ankylosis. What is the BEST initial biologic?

explanation.whyCorrect: Ankylosing spondylitis (axial spondyloarthritis): Anti-TNF (adalimumab, infliximab, etanercept, golimumab, certolizumab) is FIRST-LINE biologic after NSAID failure. Anti-IL17 (secukinumab, ixekizumab) is second-line or alternative. JAK inhibitors are for TNF failure.

whyWrong.C: JAK inhibitors for refractory disease (third-line).
whyWrong.D: Abatacept is not effective for axial disease (works in peripheral PsA).
whyWrong.E: Rituximab has NO role in SpA.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Axial SpA: NSAIDs first → Anti-TNF (first-line biologic) → Anti-IL17 (second-line) → JAK inhibitor (third-line).

vignette: A 72-year-old female presents with new-onset headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, and visual changes (blurred vision). ESR is 110 mm/hr. What is the MOST critical next step?

options.text: Temporal artery biopsy first, then start steroids
MRI brain to rule out stroke

explanation.whyCorrect: GCA with visual symptoms is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY! Start high-dose steroids IMMEDIATELY to prevent irreversible blindness. Temporal artery biopsy should be done within 2 weeks of starting steroids. Do NOT delay steroids for biopsy. Tocilizumab is steroid-sparing for maintenance.

whyWrong.B: Never delay steroids for biopsy when vision is threatened.
whyWrong.C: Methotrexate is steroid-sparing (second-line), not for acute management.
whyWrong.D: MRI may be normal in GCA; do not delay steroids for imaging.
whyWrong.E: Tocilizumab is for maintenance, not acute management.

explanation.clinicalPearl: GCA + Visual symptoms = EMERGENCY! Start steroids IMMEDIATELY (before biopsy). Irreversible blindness can occur within hours.

vignette: A 65-year-old obese female presents with knee pain that worsens with activity and improves with rest. She has morning stiffness lasting 15 minutes. X-rays show joint space narrowing and osteophytes. What is the BEST first-line treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Knee OA: First-line = Non-pharmacologic (weight loss, exercise, physical therapy, biomechanical interventions) + Acetaminophen or topical NSAIDs. Oral NSAIDs if inadequate. Intra-articular steroids for acute flares. Surgery for end-stage refractory to conservative therapy.

whyWrong.A: Surgery is last resort after conservative measures fail.
whyWrong.C: IA steroids for acute flares, not first-line maintenance.
whyWrong.D: Hyaluronic acid has limited evidence, not recommended by major guidelines.
whyWrong.E: Opioids should be AVOIDED for chronic OA (addiction risk, side effects).

explanation.clinicalPearl: OA: Weight loss + Exercise + Acetaminophen/NSAIDs. Joint replacement for end-stage refractory disease.

vignette: A 35-year-old female with SLE presents with recurrent miscarriages (3 first-trimester losses) and a DVT. Labs: Lupus anticoagulant positive on 2 occasions 12 weeks apart. What is the BEST management during her next pregnancy?

options.text: Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) + Low-dose aspirin

explanation.whyCorrect: Obstetric APS (recurrent pregnancy loss + thrombosis + positive LA): LMWH + Low-dose aspirin during pregnancy and 6 weeks postpartum. Reduces pregnancy loss from 80% to 20-30%. Warfarin is TERATOGENIC (avoid in pregnancy). Continue aspirin lifelong for thrombotic risk.

whyWrong.A: Aspirin alone is insufficient for obstetric APS with thrombosis.
whyWrong.C: Warfarin is contraindicated in pregnancy (embryopathy in 1st trimester).
whyWrong.D: Untreated obstetric APS has 80% pregnancy loss rate.
whyWrong.E: Clopidogrel is not standard for APS.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Obstetric APS: LMWH + Aspirin during pregnancy. Warfarin contraindicated. Dramatically improves live birth rates.

vignette: A 70-year-old female presents with bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain and stiffness for 1 month. Morning stiffness lasts 2 hours. She has low-grade fever and fatigue. ESR is 85 mm/hr. What is the BEST initial treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: PMR: Bilateral shoulder/hip girdle pain + Morning stiffness + Elevated ESR/CRP + Age >50 + Rapid response to low-dose steroids (prednisone 12.5-25mg daily). Dramatic improvement within 24-72 hours is diagnostic. Taper slowly over 12-18 months. Rule out GCA (ask about headache, vision, jaw claudication).

whyWrong.A: NSAIDs provide minimal relief for PMR.
whyWrong.C: Methotrexate is steroid-sparing for refractory or relapsing PMR.
whyWrong.D: Tocilizumab is for refractory PMR (third-line).
whyWrong.E: Hydroxychloroquine has no role in PMR.

explanation.clinicalPearl: PMR: Shoulder/hip girdle pain + High ESR + Age >50 + Dramatic response to low-dose prednisone. Rule out GCA!

vignette: A 50-year-old female presents with dry eyes, dry mouth, and parotid gland enlargement. She has difficulty swallowing dry food. Labs: ANA positive, Anti-SSA (Ro) positive, Anti-SSB (La) positive. What is the MOST serious complication to monitor?

explanation.whyCorrect: Sjögren syndrome: Lymphoma risk is 16-44x higher than general population. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma (usually MALT type) in parotid glands. Warning signs: Persistent parotid enlargement, splenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, cryoglobulinemia, low C4. Monitor clinically.

whyWrong.A: ILD occurs but is not the most serious complication.
whyWrong.C: RTA type 1 occurs in Sjögren but is manageable.
whyWrong.D: Peripheral neuropathy is common but not life-threatening.
whyWrong.E: PBC can co-exist but is not the most serious.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Sjögren: Highest lymphoma risk among autoimmune diseases (16-44x). Monitor for persistent parotid swelling, splenomegaly, lymphadenopathy.

vignette: A 30-year-old male from Turkey presents with recurrent painful oral ulcers (>3/year), genital ulcers, and uveitis. He also has papulopustular skin lesions and a positive pathergy test. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Behçet disease: Recurrent oral ulcers + Genital ulcers + Uveitis + Skin lesions + Positive pathergy test. Associated with HLA-B51. Most common along Silk Road (Turkey, Middle East, East Asia). Treatment: Colchicine, steroids, azathioprine, TNF inhibitors for severe.

whyWrong.A: Crohn: GI symptoms, fistulas, not oral/genital ulcers as primary.
whyWrong.C: Reactive arthritis: Urethritis + Conjunctivitis + Arthritis (not ulcers).
whyWrong.D: Pemphigus: Flaccid blisters, not aphthous ulcers.
whyWrong.E: Erythema multiforme: Target lesions, not recurrent oral/genital ulcers.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Behçet: Oral + Genital ulcers + Uveitis + Pathergy + HLA-B51. Colchicine first-line for mucocutaneous.

vignette: A 40-year-old female presents with chronic widespread pain (>3 months), fatigue, and unrefreshing sleep. She has multiple tender points on exam. All labs (CBC, ESR, CRP, ANA, RF) are normal. What is the BEST initial treatment?

options.text: Graded exercise + Cognitive behavioral therapy + Low-dose amitriptyline or duloxetine

explanation.whyCorrect: Fibromyalgia: Multimodal approach = Exercise (graded aerobic) + CBT + Pharmacotherapy (duloxetine, milnacipran, pregabalin, or low-dose amitriptyline). Opioids should be AVOIDED (no benefit, risk of dependence). Steroids and DMARDs are ineffective (not inflammatory).

whyWrong.A: Opioids are contraindicated in fibromyalgia (ineffective, risk of hyperalgesia).
whyWrong.C: Steroids have no benefit (not inflammatory).
whyWrong.D: DMARDs have no role (no synovitis or joint damage).
whyWrong.E: Gabapentin/pregabalin are one option but multimodal approach is superior.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Fibromyalgia: Exercise + CBT + Neuropathic pain medications. Avoid opioids and steroids. Normal labs help differentiate from inflammatory arthritis.

vignette: A 55-year-old female presents with Raynaud phenomenon, puffy fingers, and positive anti-centromere antibodies. What is the MOST likely subtype and associated complication?

explanation.whyCorrect: Limited scleroderma (CREST): Calcinosis + Raynaud + Esophageal dysmotility + Sclerodactyly + Telangiectasias. Anti-centromere antibody. HIGH RISK for PAH (pulmonary arterial hypertension). Screen with annual PFT + Echo. Diffuse SSc has anti-Scl70 and risk for ILD and renal crisis.

whyWrong.A: Diffuse SSc has anti-Scl70, risk for ILD, not anti-centromere.
whyWrong.C: Renal crisis is more common in diffuse SSc with anti-RNA pol III.
whyWrong.D: Esophageal dysmotility occurs in both subtypes.
whyWrong.E: SLE has different autoantibodies (anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith).

explanation.clinicalPearl: CREST (limited SSc): Anti-centromere + PAH risk. Diffuse SSc: Anti-Scl70 + ILD risk. Screen PAH annually in limited SSc.

vignette: A 30-year-old female with SLE has proteinuria 2.5g/day, active urinary sediment (RBC casts), and elevated anti-dsDNA with low C3/C4. Renal biopsy shows diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis (Class IV). What is the BEST maintenance therapy after induction?

explanation.whyCorrect: Class IV lupus nephritis: Induction (MMF or CYC + Steroids) → Maintenance (MMF or AZA). MMF is preferred (ALMS maintenance trial). Duration: At least 3-5 years of maintenance. Taper steroids slowly. Monitor urine protein, renal function, serology.

whyWrong.A: Prednisone alone is insufficient for maintenance.
whyWrong.C: Cyclophosphamide is for INDUCTION, not maintenance (toxicity).
whyWrong.D: Rituximab for refractory (not first-line maintenance).
whyWrong.E: Belimumab is add-on, not monotherapy for nephritis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Lupus nephritis maintenance: MMF or AZA x3-5 years after induction. Taper steroids slowly. Monitor proteinuria and renal function.

vignette: A 70-year-old male with knee osteoarthritis presents with acute onset monoarticular knee pain, swelling, erythema, and fever (39.0°C). Joint aspiration shows WBC 85,000/μL (95% neutrophils). Gram stain shows gram-positive cocci in clusters. What is the BEST management?

explanation.whyCorrect: Septic arthritis (S. aureus): WBC >50K (often >100K), >90% neutrophils, gram-positive cocci clusters. EMERGENCY! IV Vancomycin (MRSA coverage) + EMERGENT surgical drainage/washout. Delay in drainage destroys cartilage within 24-48h. Serial aspirations if surgery not available.

whyWrong.A: Septic arthritis requires IV antibiotics and drainage, not oral outpatient.
whyWrong.C: Intra-articular antibiotics are unnecessary and may cause chemical synovitis.
whyWrong.D: Ceftriaxone does not cover MRSA or all gram-positives adequately.
whyWrong.E: Steroids would worsen infection.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Septic arthritis: EMERGENCY! IV antibiotics + Surgical drainage. WBC >50K with >90% neutrophils. S. aureus most common.

vignette: A 75-year-old female presents with acute knee pain, swelling, and erythema. Joint aspiration shows positively birefringent rhomboid-shaped crystals. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Pseudogout (CPPD arthropathy): Calcium pyrophosphate crystals = POSITIVELY birefringent, rhomboid-shaped. Gout: Negatively birefringent, needle-shaped (monosodium urate). Treatment: NSAIDs, Colchicine (less effective than gout), intra-articular steroids.

whyWrong.A: Gout: Negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals (urate).
whyWrong.C: Septic arthritis: WBC >50K, positive Gram stain/culture.
whyWrong.D: OA: Non-inflammatory, no crystals on aspiration.
whyWrong.E: RA: Inflammatory, no crystals, RF/anti-CCP positive.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Gout: Negative birefringence, needle-shaped. Pseudogout: POSITIVE birefringence, rhomboid-shaped. Remember: 'P'ositive = 'P'seudogout.

vignette: A 25-year-old male presents with asymmetric oligoarthritis (knees, ankles), conjunctivitis, and dysuria 2 weeks after a diarrheal illness. He is HLA-B27 positive. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Reactive arthritis: 'Can't see, can't pee, can't bend the knee.' Triad: Arthritis + Conjunctivitis/Uveitis + Urethritis. Post-infectious (Chlamydia, Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia). HLA-B27 associated. Treatment: NSAIDs, treat underlying infection. Steroids/DMARDs if severe.

whyWrong.A: Gonococcal: Migratory polyarthralgia, tenosynovitis, pustular skin lesions.
whyWrong.C: RA: Symmetric, MCP/PIP, RF/anti-CCP positive.
whyWrong.D: AS: Axial involvement (spine/SI joints), not peripheral with conjunctivitis/urethritis.
whyWrong.E: PsA: Skin psoriasis, nail changes, dactylitis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Reactive arthritis: Arthritis + Conjunctivitis + Urethritis post-infection. HLA-B27. NSAIDs first-line.

vignette: A 50-year-old male presents with chronic sinusitis, cough with hemoptysis, and hematuria with RBC casts. CXR shows bilateral nodules and cavitary lesions. c-ANCA (PR3) is positive. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: GPA (Wegener): Upper respiratory (sinusitis) + Lower respiratory (nodules, hemoptysis) + Renal (RPGN with RBC casts) + c-ANCA (PR3). Treatment: Steroids + Cyclophosphamide or Rituximab (RAVE trial). Life-threatening without treatment.

whyWrong.A: Goodpasture: Anti-GBM antibody, pulmonary hemorrhage + GN, no sinus involvement.
whyWrong.C: EGPA (Churg-Strauss): Asthma + Eosinophilia + p-ANCA (MPO).
whyWrong.D: MPA: p-ANCA (MPO), no granulomas, no sinus involvement.
whyWrong.E: Sarcoidosis: BHL, non-caseating granulomas, no hemoptysis or RPGN.

explanation.clinicalPearl: GPA: Sinus + Lung + Kidney + c-ANCA (PR3). Treatment: Rituximab or Cyclophosphamide + Steroids.

vignette: A 60-year-old diabetic male with a foot ulcer that probes to bone. MRI shows osteomyelitis of the 1st metatarsal. What is the BEST diagnostic test?

options.text: Bone biopsy with culture and histopathology

explanation.whyCorrect: Osteomyelitis: Bone biopsy is the GOLD STANDARD for diagnosis and identifying the causative organism. Culture guides antibiotic therapy (6 weeks). MRI is excellent for diagnosis but does not provide microbiology. Wound swabs correlate poorly with bone pathogens.

whyWrong.A: MRI cannot identify the organism or antibiotic sensitivities.
whyWrong.C: Wound swabs have poor correlation with bone cultures.
whyWrong.D: X-ray has low sensitivity (changes take 2-4 weeks).
whyWrong.E: Blood cultures positive in only 25-50%.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Osteomyelitis: Bone biopsy = Gold standard. Guides 6 weeks of targeted antibiotics. Wound swabs are unreliable.

vignette: A 30-year-old African American female presents with cough, dyspnea, erythema nodosum, and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on CXR. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Sarcoidosis: BHL + Erythema nodosum + African American female = Lofgren syndrome (good prognosis). Non-caseating granulomas on biopsy. Elevated ACE levels (not diagnostic). Treatment: NSAIDs for mild, Prednisone for pulmonary/ocular/cardiac involvement.

whyWrong.A: TB: Cavitary lesions, caseating granulomas, positive AFB.
whyWrong.C: Lymphoma: Anterior mediastinal mass, not symmetric BHL.
whyWrong.D: Histoplasmosis: Calcified granulomas, not BHL with erythema nodosum.
whyWrong.E: Lung cancer: Mass, not symmetric BHL.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Lofgren syndrome: BHL + Erythema nodosum = Sarcoidosis with excellent prognosis. NSAIDs for symptom control.

vignette: A 50-year-old male presents with progressive proximal muscle weakness (difficulty standing from chair) and elevated CK (3000). He has NO rash. EMG shows myopathic changes. Anti-Jo-1 antibody is positive. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Polymyositis: Proximal weakness + Elevated CK + Anti-Jo-1 (anti-synthetase syndrome) + NO rash. If rash present (Gottron, heliotrope) = Dermatomyositis. Anti-Jo-1 associated with ILD, mechanic's hands, Raynaud, arthritis. Treatment: Steroids + Methotrexate or Azathioprine.

whyWrong.A: Dermatomyositis has characteristic rash.
whyWrong.C: IBM: Distal > proximal, forearm flexors, CK normal-mild.
whyWrong.D: Statin myopathy: Improves with discontinuation, CK moderately elevated.
whyWrong.E: PMR: Shoulder/hip girdle pain, normal CK, age >50, responds to low-dose steroids.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Polymyositis: Proximal weakness + High CK + Anti-Jo1 + No rash. Dermatomyositis: Same + Characteristic rash.

vignette: A 30-year-old female presents with daily spiking fevers (>39°C), evanescent salmon-pink rash, arthritis, and sore throat. Labs: Leukocytosis, elevated ferritin (>5000), negative ANA and RF. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Adult-onset Still disease: Yamaguchi criteria. Daily spiking fevers + Salmon-pink evanescent rash (appears with fever) + Arthritis + Sore throat + Leukocytosis + Ferritin >1000 (often >5000) + Negative ANA/RF. Treatment: NSAIDs, steroids, anakinra (IL-1 inhibitor) for refractory.

whyWrong.A: SLE: Malar rash, ANA/dsDNA positive, not daily spiking fevers.
whyWrong.C: RA: Symmetric polyarthritis, RF/anti-CCP positive, no spiking fevers.
whyWrong.D: Gout: Acute monoarthritis, no systemic symptoms.
whyWrong.E: Septic: Monoarthritis with positive cultures.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Still disease: Spiking fevers + Salmon-pink rash + Ferritin >5000. Anakinra (anti-IL1) for refractory. Rule out HLH.

vignette: A 50-year-old male with chronic HCV presents with palpable purpura on his legs, peripheral neuropathy, and glomerulonephritis (hematuria, proteinuria). C4 is low, C3 is normal. Rheumatoid factor is positive. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Mixed cryoglobulinemia (Type II): HCV-associated. Triad: Palpable purpura + Peripheral neuropathy + Glomerulonephritis (MPGN pattern). Labs: Low C4 (classic pathway activation), normal C3, positive RF (IgM anti-IgG). Treatment: Treat underlying HCV (DAA therapy) + Immunosuppression if severe.

whyWrong.A: HSP: IgA deposition, follows URI, more common in children.
whyWrong.C: GPA: c-ANCA/PR3 positive, sinus/lung involvement.
whyWrong.D: MPA: p-ANCA/MPO positive, no HCV association.
whyWrong.E: SLE: Low C3 AND C4, ANA/dsDNA positive.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Cryoglobulinemia: HCV + Purpura + Neuropathy + GN + Low C4 (normal C3) + Positive RF. Treat HCV first!

vignette: A 25-year-old female presents with hypermobile joints, skin hyperextensibility, and atrophic scarring. She has a history of joint dislocations. What type of EDS is this?

explanation.whyCorrect: Classical EDS (COL5A1, COL5A2): Skin hyperextensibility + Atrophic scarring + Joint hypermobility. Vascular EDS (COL3A1): Thin translucent skin, arterial/organ rupture, characteristic facies. Hypermobile EDS: Joint hypermobility without significant skin findings. No cure. Avoid contact sports.

whyWrong.A: Vascular: Arterial rupture, thin skin, no atrophic scarring.
whyWrong.C: Hypermobile: Joint hypermobility, minimal skin involvement.
whyWrong.D: Kyphoscoliotic: Severe scoliosis, ocular fragility.
whyWrong.E: Arthrochalasia: Congenital hip dislocation.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Classical EDS: Skin hyperextensibility + Atrophic scars + Joint hypermobility. COL5A1/COL5A2. Vascular EDS: COL3A1, arterial rupture.
